title: Your dominant hand is made, not born
gist: A new PNAS study led by SFI's John Krakauer (with Ahmet Arac and Nicolas Y. H. Jeong Lee, UCLA) finds that hand 'dominance' — unlike hand 'preference' — is not innate but emerges from a lifetime of tool-mediated practice, appearing only in tasks requiring complex trajectory control and vanishing when tested with an unpracticed effector such as an elbow-strapped pen.

insights.claim: A new PNAS study led by John Krakauer (Johns Hopkins) distinguishes hand 'preference' — which hand people instinctively favor, present before birth with biological roots — from hand 'dominance' — the skill gap between hands — arguing only preference is innate.
insights.speaker: John Krakauer
insights.so_what: Overturns a foundational assumption in motor neuroscience by separating two commonly conflated constructs, relevant to any research or product built on 'dominant hemisphere' motor-control models.
insights.locator: separates two ideas — preference and dominance

insights.claim: Using 3D motion capture, researchers found no measurable skill advantage for the dominant arm during ordinary reaching or reaching with an added wrist weight; the dominance gap appeared only when subjects used a lightweight stick fixed to the forearm that required navigating a complex, curved path.
insights.speaker: John Krakauer
insights.so_what: Localizes the mechanism of hand dominance to tool-mediated complex trajectory control rather than raw motor capability, a concrete falsifiable finding for follow-up motor-control research.
insights.locator: In a series of experiments using 3D motion capture

insights.claim: When participants wrote using a pen strapped to the elbow, removing the normal hand's dexterity advantage, hand dominance vanished entirely: both elbows improved equally with equal practice, and each ended up outperforming the untrained nondominant hand.
insights.speaker: John Krakauer
insights.so_what: The strongest single piece of evidence that dominance is trained rather than innate — practice, not biology, explains the skill gap.
insights.locator: when participants wrote with their elbows

insights.claim: John Krakauer argues causally that people don't prefer their dominant hand because it's more skilled — the hand becomes more skilled because it is preferred and therefore practiced more, with the gap only detectable through tool use that demands precise practice.
insights.speaker: John Krakauer
insights.so_what: Reframes cause and effect in a widely-assumed biological trait, a template for reassessing other 'innate' human skill differences as practice-driven rather than hardwired.
insights.quote: It becomes more skilled because you prefer it.

insights.claim: Study co-author Ahmet Arac argues that because humans are uniquely prolific tool users and makers, handedness may be a byproduct of human inventiveness — a 'fingerprint of human tool-use culture' rather than a fixed biological trait.
insights.speaker: Ahmet Arac
insights.so_what: Positions handedness as a marker of cultural and technological practice rather than pure biology, relevant to research on human-tool co-evolution and skill acquisition.
insights.quote: Since humans are uniquely prolific tool users and makers, handedness may be a byproduct of our inventiveness.
insights.locator: "Since humans are uniquely prolific tool users

insights.claim: The study frames arm dominance as an emergent trait — not stored in a single gene or brain hemisphere, but accumulating from a lifetime of practice with self-invented tools, arising from the interplay of biology, behavior, and culture.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: A concrete real-world case study in 'emergence' from SFI's complexity-science research agenda, a template for studying other seemingly-fixed human traits.
insights.locator: The study reframes arm dominance as something that emerges

insights.claim: The researchers propose studying populations where hand preference and practice diverge — left-handers forced to use tools right-handed, stroke survivors whose hand preference shifts, and amputees developing skill with unconventional prosthetic effectors — as natural experiments to further test the practice-driven-dominance hypothesis.
insights.speaker: John Krakauer
insights.so_what: Flags concrete future research directions and clinical populations, e.g. stroke rehabilitation and prosthetics, where this framework could be tested and applied.
insights.locator: They point to a natural next step

insights.claim: The findings are published as 'Arm dominance is an emergent effect of practice executing complex trajectory shapes required by tools and objects' by Ahmet Arac, Nicolas Y. H. Jeong Lee, and John Krakauer in PNAS (DOI: 10.1073/pnas.2601569123).
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Primary source citation for verifying and following up on the underlying peer-reviewed study.
insights.locator: Read the paper, "Arm dominance is an emergent effect
